item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image could not be properly sourced, and is assumed to be non-compliant. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. Would be interesting to see what could be learned from any kind of medical waste recovered. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If caring whether the dump moves the item is enough for it not to. Shouldn't anything they watch (like the control items) be stuck?

Seems almost like there is another explanation here but I can't put my finger on it.

What if they just started throwing standard garbage in it with no intention of keeping track of it? Enough of it builds up in there and they should notice if that garbage never leaves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will now commence to answer a nerd-tastic question asked almost exactly five years ago.
Nothing to see here, move along.

Let's assume there is a fourth direction, a W axis along with the X, Y, and Z we are used to.
Our universe is every combination of X, Y, and Z where W=0.
Every other possible value of W would have its own complete set of X, Y, and Z points. So every "alternate dimension" in this "extra-dimensional" set would be equally large.
Which leaves us with an effectively infinite number of universes, each comparable in scale to our own.

In an infinite number of universes, the laws of probability dictate that anything that is even remotely possibly will happen with 100% certainty.
So not only is there a universe where the coin flip turned up heads, your parents never met, and the Cubs won the 1736 Heismann Trophy for Best Picture; there is also an infinite number where the Big Bang happened just a little bit differently and the laws of nature aren't the same as we know them.

I'm not saying it's not an overused trope, it certainly is. But alternate dimension as a stand-in for alternate timeline or reality is, at least mathematically, theoretically sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The Manichean religion is largely considered to be extinct.. "

I'm not sure if ideas or belief systems can become extinct. This might read better if described as going "unpracticed" or something else?

+1 for interdimensional detritus tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as the nature of the items found in SCP-1330 and additional tests confirmed.

sorry if this seems nitpicky, but I don't think this phrase is necessary, that they tested it is ~implied

overall, I like the concept but something about the writing seems off to me. like it's trying to be technical/scientific but not quite getting there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for foiling the Foundation's increasingly elaborate research attempts with what was probably just a safety feature to prevent loss of possessions / body parts / employees.

That said, I was a bit disappointed by the experiment logs as a whole. Every attempt to outsmart it basically boiled down to "tell someone else to do it," with extra levels of bureaucracy added each time. Even if the anomaly can't detect intentions from the chain of command, any Foundation employee involved in that process is going to suspect something beyond everyday garbage disposal. As long as someone is wondering "What was so important about that tin can?", the dump would probably hang onto it just in case.

I can understand not wanting the Foundation to fully master this anomaly, but the current test records haven't convinced me that this thing is research-proof. Especially since the final test indicates that research is ongoing and "of the highest priority."

TL;DR: Could use more experiment logs, and I have some ideas. Figured I should start by asking the author about it.
Oh, this crossed 100. Took it a while, but I'm glad to see it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite fantastic. So many great details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted when I realized that it's an interdimensional New Jersey.

Unified Western Manichean Church

Downvoted, then upvoted again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.

It's now part of my headcanon that if you go too far into the landfill, you'll wind up in SCP-967.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love extradimensional SCPs. Upvoted. Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed more than I should have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This might be one of my favorite non-horrifying SCPs. I think it's really hard to find an angle on an "infinitely producing weird/creative/quirky/freaky things" SCP that hasn't been done better already, but you managed it pretty handily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh cool, the super-sargasso sea concept has finally become an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't you already do that with your last edit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you probably saw it, it was SCP-1464, it lasted two days, it was hugely controversial.

I've removed the allusion because it made me uncomfortable and because the subject of the in-joke is dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to see that…if you do please do link it up to the 887 log if you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still intend to make that one a mainlist SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was so entirely inappropriate it's impossible to repeat here fully; suffice to say it involved a man who could bring people back to life, but some facts of his character (namely, his illegal sexual fetish) and the way the resurrection process was performed brought him a large amount of discomfort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I've made a few changes to match the context of that log and put it with a set of other (unrelated) items I've added to that list. I do like Presidential Perversions too, though, and I can see it makes more sense for a rubbish dump.

I'm curious now: what was it an in-joke about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fine. The item was replaced by "Presidential Perversions" by Voct, with my permission. It actually started as a rather silly in-joke on the IRC chat, and I figured this one had more staying power. It would be nice to see it somewhere else, and the test log is as good a place as any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a note-I really rather liked one of the items on the list ('The Sorrowful Lover of Death') that's been removed from this page. If nobody minds I've now put it in the log for SCP-887 with attribution in the edits log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a little puzzled by that for another reason: I interpreted it to mean 'AM/FM radio', which to my knowledge is not standard issue on modern cellphones (it's largely obsolete anyway if the phone has internet access as most radio stations also stream their content online).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My complaint is specifically the use of "extra-dimensional" a synonym for "from an alternate timeline or reality". That's not covered by your explanation, is it?1

Footnotes
1. This is not a barb, I'm sincerely asking. I don't think it was addressed, but I'd like to be sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually I don't mind minor fixes, but those were too extensive for my taste, so I reverted them back. Most of those were less grammar issues and more phrasing, and after checking it out I prefer my own. No hard feelings or anything, but you should know this sort of change goes a bit beyond what is normally acceptable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't hear back from you, so I edited. Was that all right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The word "dimension" has multiple, equally valid meanings. Don't get hung up on the "axis of movement" one and it all makes more sense.
For example, "3 dimensional" means "having extent in 3 axes" so "extra-dimensional" could easily be taken to mean "having extent in an unknown number of axes greater than typically observed".

Similarly, "the 3rd dimension" is often used to refer to all locations that require coordinates on 3 distinct axes to individually distinguish. So, "an alternate dimension" would just be the set of all points that require some alternate coordinate system to define. Technically, this would make 2-D fields "alternate dimensions" as much as higher levels.

This unsolicitied ramble brought to you by the Association of Over-Educated Math Nerds. We now return you to reading something anyone actually cares about.

EDIT: Huh, deja vu, hard. Have I gone off on this tangent before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A landfill SCP? Never seen that one before.

Extradimensional landfill? Nice.

Descriptions of items in the item log? Great.

+1

(I have no idea why would the foundation want to deliberately insert our items into another universe, though.)

Dmatix, once again a great SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know that well-intentioned sci-fi authors have made this pet peeve of mine hilariously futile, but "extra-dimensional" /"alternate dimensions" is really not what you mean here, especially not in a pseudoscientific document…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I bet this is where all the shit that the Foundation pumps into D.B. Cooper's Mouth-Handbag ends up as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for possibly the most interesting pile of garbage ever.

One small nitpick: "the device lacks features common in commercial cellphones such as a camera or a radio." Surely a cellphone by definition contains a radio (even if non-functioning)? Possibly you meant wi-fi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With your permission, I'd like to clean up the few typos and grammatical errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, no, I'm pretty sure that description fits whoever left the diamonds there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, the idea went through a lot of feedback in the chat, and I suspect it's only because of that that it turned up well.

Also, you have a sometime habit of liking my stuff and forgetting to upvote. I sense a conspiracy afoot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the extents to which the Foundation is willing to go to to try and outwit a garbage dump. I also like what you're saying regarding the subjective worth of the items the Foundation are trying to send. This is well written, and I think is an interesting concept executed very well. You sir, get a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't expect to like this…but I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this so much. With all the dimension-hopping that goes on in the Foundationverse, they all gotta have trash somewhere.

Also, Conqueror Worm Energy Drink and Golgotha, Ohio made me try to upvote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful. If it wasn't for the issue of crossing over with another SCP, I would like to see a piece of trash from the vending machine. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to tie this to Spring Cleaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People throw away stuff which might not be objectively worthless, but it is to them. The reason non of the Foundation's items pass through is that they are interested in them passing, and by doing so giving them worth.

Well reasoned, and well said. I really like the concept of this SCP and the underlying thought process. Makes me wonder about what sorts of "trash" people generate, and all the different things people would regard as "useless".

The reason why the last log is long is to show the Foundation becoming increasingly desperate in their attempts to outwit what is basically a pile of garbage, and failing.

This is an excellent sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. I like this a lot. The idea at the heart of it is just a bumper sticker aphorism, but playing it for genre actually adds a fair amount of depth, enables some wry goofiness, makes interesting commentaries on politics and consumerism, and uses the context of the Foundation well. It's effective on several levels without taking itself too seriously. The testing is well-executed and communicates the frustration of the people dealing with it very simply. All in all, a damn good piece of work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most are, but not all of them. This thing will accept anything considered worthless- this includes a book you got but never wanted, an old newspaper, or a family album from a family which might not exist anymore, in one way or another. People throw away stuff which might not be objectively worthless, but it is to them. The reason non of the Foundation's items pass through is that they are interested in them passing, and by doing so giving them worth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't all the recovered items be horribly damaged or otherwise ruined or useless in some way or other?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The more I think about this one the more I like it. The pile only accepts items that are worthless but attempting to observe an item assigns worth. Brilliant! Very Douglas Adams-esque. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The latter. The reason why the last log is long is to show the Foundation becoming increasingly desperate in their attempts to outwit what is basically a pile of garbage, and failing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept (and the diamonds made me giggle).

Some of the paragraphs could be broken up into multiple paragraphs (especially the containment procedures).

and stored in the appropriate chamber

How would someone reading this know which chamber is the appropriate one?

I think the second log, where they try to get the dump to accept anything, is a little too rambling - it has too many entries to get to the final point, which seems to be "The dump won't accept anything somebody intends for it to accept, regardless of the source of that intent."

Is the reason it won't accept the research items because it refuses intentional dumpings, or because the information hoped to be gained from the research attaches some value to the items, making them not worthless?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very happy to sit down with a can of Conqueror Worm Energy Drink, a sandwich of Murray's Marvelous Marmite, and play laser-pointer-chase with my saber-toothed kitty. In fact if I had to envision a physical heaven, that would occur for at *least* a good year or so for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me too, I think if there was a collaborative log on this one I would enjoy it a lot less. At least be sure to put something like that off page where it doesn't ruin the original piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I too enjoyed this article, but I'm not sure I agree about the collective log. I kind of like this one the way it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1330 appears to only affect items discarded as being worthless,

How did the researchers come to that conclusion? From the article, it sounds like the only conclusion they could logically come to is that it won't affect an item if anyone wants the item to be affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This.

We've already got a toilet bowl of diamonds, I'd love to see what other trash 27 universes worth of people throw away.

EDIT: And I just now got the implication of the toilet bowl full of diamonds. Sometimes I'm just the densest person ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent. I'd love it if it had a collaborative recovery log on another page along the lines of 261 and the other classics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: TIME TO GO DUMPSTER-DIVING

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dimensions of infinite trash?

The plane of infinite kittens can go suck it!

Anyway, I like that it can sense the intent of anyone involved in trash, and the ideas of dimensions of infinite trash amuses me.

Upvoted
